meta.title: Rawana Ceylon - Tourist Law, Ethics & Safety Notification Dataset v2
meta.disclaimer: General travel-safety and cultural-etiquette information compiled from tourism operators, travel-safety publications and secondary legal summaries - NOT the Penal Code/Gazette directly. Cross-check exact penalties against sltda.gov.lk, immigration.gov.lk, caa.lk, travel.state.gov and gov.uk/foreign-travel-advice/sri-lanka before publishing precise figures in-app.

entries.title: Never pose with your back to a Buddha statue
entries.explanation: Treated as desecration of a sacred image, not bad manners. People have been arrested and deported for this.

entries.title: Never touch, sit on, or climb a Buddha statue
entries.explanation: Physical contact with a Buddha image is a criminal offense under religious-artifact protection.

entries.title: Cover Buddha-image tattoos while in the country
entries.explanation: Visible Buddha tattoos have led to visitors being denied entry or deported (2012 French tourist case).

entries.title: Don't wear Buddha-image clothing or accessories as fashion
entries.explanation: Buddha imagery used as decoration is treated as disrespect toward a sacred symbol.

entries.title: Never damage or mistreat any Buddhist image or artifact
entries.explanation: This is a criminal offense anywhere in the country, not just inside temples.

entries.title: Remove shoes before entering temple grounds
entries.explanation: Footwear (and sometimes hats) must be removed at the outer wall, not just the inner shrine.

entries.title: Don't point your feet at a Buddha statue or monk
entries.explanation: Feet are considered the lowest/impure part of the body in South and Southeast Asian Buddhist custom.

entries.title: Don't touch monks, especially their heads, and keep distance from monks generally (applies doubly to women)
entries.explanation: Monks observe vows of physical separation from laypeople, particularly from women; unsolicited contact is a serious breach.

entries.title: No photography where temple signage prohibits it
entries.explanation: Many inner shrine rooms and museum areas ban photography to protect murals/artifacts and worship privacy.

entries.title: No flash photography inside cave temples or shrine rooms
entries.explanation: Flash light damages centuries-old murals and paintings over repeated exposure.

entries.title: Dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered) throughout the Sacred City zone, not just inside shrines
entries.explanation: Anuradhapura's core is a continuously consecrated sacred area; modest dress is expected the moment you enter, not only at the stupa steps.

entries.title: No leather items (belts, watch straps, bags) near the Sri Maha Bodhi enclosure
entries.explanation: Leather is considered ritually impure in the innermost sacred enclosures.

entries.title: No motorized vehicles inside the inner Sacred City core - cycle or walk between monuments
entries.explanation: The Sacred City core restricts vehicle access to protect the archaeological zone and to preserve the pilgrimage atmosphere.

entries.title: Circumambulate stupas and shrines clockwise (keep the object on your right)
entries.explanation: Clockwise circling (pradakshina) is the customary direction of respect around a sacred structure.

entries.title: Speak quietly and avoid loud group behavior near the Sri Maha Bodhi and Ruwanwelisaya
entries.explanation: These are active, continuously worshipped pilgrimage sites, not just archaeological ruins.

entries.title: Don't climb on stupa brickwork or ruins for photos
entries.explanation: Climbing on protected archaeological monuments is prohibited and can damage 2,000+ year old structures.

entries.title: Alcohol sale is banned nationwide on Poya (full moon) days
entries.explanation: Bars, restaurants, bottle shops, supermarkets and hotel bars all stop alcohol sales - this covers ~12-13 days/year.
entries.penalty: Refusal of service; venues risk license suspension

entries.title: Drinking alcohol you already purchased is legal in private (hotel room, minibar)
entries.explanation: The Poya law bans sale, not private consumption of already-owned alcohol.

entries.title: Major Poya days (e.g. Vesak) can extend the dry period to 2-3 days and also close casinos/betting shops/nightclubs
entries.explanation: Vesak and other major religious Poyas trigger extended restrictions beyond the usual single day.

entries.title: Alcohol sale is also restricted on Independence Day (Feb 4), Sinhala/Tamil New Year (Apr 13-14), Christmas Day, and some Hindu/Christian holy days
Drinking or smoking in public places (streets, parks, buses) is prohibited
entries.explanation: Legal inside bars, restaurants, and private venues, but not in open public spaces.

entries.title: Meat, especially red meat, may be unavailable outside tourist areas on major Poya days
entries.explanation: Abattoirs are officially ordered shut on major Poya days such as Vesak.

entries.title: A foreign license or ordinary IDP alone is NOT sufficient to drive in Sri Lanka
entries.explanation: You need a 1968 Vienna Convention IDP, an AA-Ceylon-endorsed 1949 Geneva IDP recognition permit, or a Sri Lankan Temporary Driving Licence.
entries.penalty: Vehicle seizure, arrest, denied insurance claims

entries.title: Temporary Driving Licences are issued on-arrival at Bandaranaike International Airport
entries.explanation: Available since August 2025 at BIA, or at the DMT Werahera office, for those without a qualifying IDP.

entries.title: Tuk-tuks (three-wheelers) require a separate endorsement beyond a car-covering permit
entries.explanation: An IDP/permit valid for cars does not automatically authorize tuk-tuk driving.

entries.title: Sri Lanka drives on the LEFT; minimum self-drive rental age is usually 21
Self-drive safaris are prohibited inside all national parks - a licensed jeep/guide is mandatory
entries.explanation: Applies at Yala, Wilpattu, Udawalawe, Gal Oya, Horton Plains and all other national parks.

entries.title: No photos of military installations, checkpoints, government buildings, or VIP convoys
entries.explanation: Enforced with fines or worse; the northern/eastern former conflict zones still have an active military presence.

entries.title: No photography inside most government institutions
Avoid photographing checkpoints or soldiers in the North and East even casually
entries.explanation: This region retains a heavier security presence and sensitivities tied to the civil war; casual tourist photos are frequently misread.

entries.title: All drones must be registered with the Civil Aviation Authority of Sri Lanka (CAASL) before flying
entries.explanation: This includes small consumer drones; only cameraless drones under ~200g may be exempt, and even those may need police notification.

entries.title: No drone flights within 5 miles of any airport
entries.explanation: Standard no-fly buffer around Bandaranaike (Katunayake), Ratmalana, and regional airports.

entries.title: No drone flights over military sites
Drone flights over Sigiriya, Dambulla, and other archaeological/religious sites need separate Department of Archaeology sign-off
Drone flights inside national parks (Yala, Wilpattu, Udawalawe etc.) need Wildlife Department clearance
entries.explanation: Uncleared drone use can disturb wildlife and violate park regulations.

entries.title: Night drone flights require an additional permit; max approved altitude is generally ~45m (150ft)
Nearly all recreational drugs, including marijuana, are illegal with no tourist/medical exception
entries.explanation: Sri Lankan law technically retains the death penalty for drug trafficking (unused since the 1970s), and life sentences are real and applied.

entries.title: Random police checks on tuk-tuks, taxis, and roadside stops are common at night, especially in party/beach towns
Same-sex sexual relations remain criminalized under a colonial-era law
entries.explanation: Enforcement against tourists is rare, but it affects safety advice: avoid public displays of affection and don't assume legal protection.

entries.title: You may not take more than a small amount of Sri Lankan Rupees (commonly cited as LKR 5,000) out of the country
entries.explanation: The Rupee is a closed/non-convertible currency.

entries.title: Declare foreign cash above USD 15,000 brought in, or above USD 10,000 taken out
E-cigarettes and vaping devices are prohibited imports
Carry prescription medication in original packaging with the prescription
entries.explanation: Counterfeit/unclear medication imports create real customs risk.

entries.title: Buy cigarettes locally - bringing large quantities in for personal use is restricted
Nudity and topless sunbathing are illegal on all Sri Lankan beaches, including at resorts
Public displays of affection (kissing, overt romantic contact) draw social and sometimes legal attention
entries.explanation: Hand-holding is generally fine; more than that is culturally conspicuous.

entries.title: Loud, aggressive public arguments are strongly socially penalized
Shoulders and knees covered at the Temple of the Sacred Tooth Relic
entries.explanation: Men in shorts are turned away from inner shrine areas.

entries.title: Shoes off and shoulders/knees covered at Dambulla Cave Temple
entries.explanation: Sarongs/scarves are rentable at the entrance if you arrive under-dressed.

entries.title: No hats or caps inside temple buildings
Leather items (belts, wallets, bags) are prohibited inside many Hindu kovils
Head covering is expected inside mosques (opposite of temple rule - cover, don't uncover)
entries.explanation: Women should carry a scarf; shoulders/legs covered for all genders.

entries.title: Self-guided/self-drive safaris are prohibited - a DWC-licensed jeep and driver is mandatory
entries.explanation: Applies at Yala, Wilpattu, Udawalawe and other national parks; only ~1/5 of Yala is even open to visitor access.

entries.title: Stay inside the jeep at all times except at designated stops
entries.explanation: This protects both visitor safety and minimizes wildlife disturbance.

entries.title: Engine off and silence during leopard/elephant sightings - no shouting, no chasing the sighting
entries.explanation: Jeep 'flocking' behaviour (30-50 vehicles converging on one radio call) causes documented wildlife stress.

entries.title: All jeeps must exit the park by 18:00 - park hours are 06:00-18:00 and strictly enforced
entries.explanation: Wildlife officers log license plates on entry and exit.

entries.title: Yala is a strict no-plastic zone - bring a reusable bottle
entries.explanation: Most operators now supply reusable glass bottles for guests.

entries.title: Never approach or feed wild elephants on roadsides near parks/villages
entries.explanation: Human-elephant conflict is a serious ongoing safety issue outside as well as inside parks.

entries.title: A passport is mandatory for national park entry - it is checked at the gate
Boats must stay at least 100m from whales and 50m from dolphins
entries.explanation: Required under Sri Lanka's Fisheries and Aquatic Resources Management Act and international marine-mammal watching guidelines.
entries.penalty: Operator fine; report unsafe operators to the Coast Guard

entries.title: Engines should idle/switch off within 100m of whales - no chasing or cutting across a whale's path
entries.explanation: Overcrowding and fast approaches by 10-25 competing boats is documented to cause acoustic stress and habitat avoidance in blue whales.

entries.title: Choose licensed, small-group operators that limit time per pod to ~30 minutes
Never touch, feed, or attempt to swim with whales or dolphins; do not discard anything into the ocean
Don't touch, pick up, or take flash photos of nesting sea turtles or hatchlings
entries.explanation: Handling stresses turtles and can prevent nesting; flash disorients hatchlings finding the sea.

entries.title: Avoid touching or standing on coral at protected reef sites
entries.explanation: Hikkaduwa and Bar Reef are gazetted marine protected areas; reef damage is a punishable offense.

entries.title: No spearfishing or removal of marine life/shells from protected marine areas
Single-use plastics (cutlery, straws, cups, artificial flowers) are banned under national law
entries.explanation: Introduced after repeated wild elephant and cattle deaths from plastic ingestion at waste dumps.

entries.title: Non-biodegradable plastic bags have been banned since 2017
Horton Plains National Park is a strict no-plastic zone
entries.explanation: Enforced to protect the cloud forest and montane grassland ecosystem around World's End and Baker's Falls.

entries.title: Do not litter on hiking trails or feed wildlife along the way (e.g. Horton Plains, Ella, Knuckles)
Open burning of rubbish, including plastics, is banned
Start Adam's Peak (Sri Pada) at night to summit for sunrise - trail lighting and crowds are built around this timing
entries.explanation: Sri Pada is a major multi-faith pilgrimage site (Buddhist, Hindu, Muslim, Christian traditions), so trail etiquette and pace matter.

entries.title: Horton Plains' World's End viewpoint can be shrouded in mist after 10am - go early, and don't cross safety barriers at the cliff edge
entries.explanation: The escarpment has a sheer ~870m drop with minimal barriers in places.

entries.title: Stay on marked trails in the Knuckles Range and Horton Plains - off-trail hiking risks getting lost and disturbs endemic species habitat
Leeches are common on hill-country and rainforest trails - wear leech socks, don't panic-pull them off
Never lean or hang out of train doors/windows for photos, especially on the Kandy-Ella-Badulla line
entries.explanation: Multiple tourists have died leaning out of moving trains for selfies on this exact route, including near the Nine Arch Bridge.

entries.title: Clear the tracks at Nine Arch Bridge the moment you hear a train horn - there are no safety barriers
entries.explanation: Up to 10 trains a day cross a bridge with no railings; the surface is also slippery when wet.

entries.title: Trespassing on railway property, including walking onto the Nine Arch Bridge itself, is technically an offense under the Railway Ordinance
entries.penalty: Fine (rarely enforced against tourists, but real)

entries.title: Don't ride on train footboards or roofs, however common it looks on social media
entries.penalty: Injury/death, and doors on older rolling stock don't lock centrally

entries.title: Elephant rides and elephant 'bathing' experiences are widely considered unethical by welfare groups
entries.explanation: Elephants used for rides are typically chained, trained with a bullhook (ankus), and can suffer spinal damage from howdah seating.

entries.title: Pinnawala Elephant Orphanage is government-run and does not officially permit rides, but privately-run elephant camps nearby do - check which operator you're booking
entries.explanation: Welfare assessments of Pinnawala itself are mixed/contested; independent camps in the same town have drawn heavier criticism.

entries.title: Elephants paraded at festivals such as the Kandy Esala Perahera are a documented animal-welfare concern
Ask before you visit whether a 'turtle sanctuary' releases hatchlings promptly to the sea rather than holding them for tourist photos
entries.explanation: Several west-coast 'turtle hatcheries' have been criticized as more show than conservation.

entries.title: Avoid snake charmers / performing-animal street photo-ops in tourist towns
Ask permission before photographing tea pluckers at work
entries.explanation: Many tea estates now have visitor centers precisely because informal photography of workers became intrusive.

entries.title: Stick to marked visitor paths on working tea estates - the fields are a workplace, not a park
Only buy gems from licensed dealers (look for National Gem and Jewellery Authority registration) and get a certificate
entries.explanation: Ratnapura is Sri Lanka's gem capital and a known hotspot for tourist-targeted fake-gem and inflated-price scams.
entries.penalty: Financial loss, no legal recourse without paperwork

entries.title: Be wary of unsolicited 'gem mine tour' offers from strangers on the street
Do not climb on, sit on, or remove pieces from ruins at Polonnaruwa or Anuradhapura
entries.explanation: Protected under the Antiquities Ordinance; these are UNESCO World Heritage sites.

entries.title: Sigiriya's summit and frescoes area have restricted photography zones and no-touch rules on the ancient plaster murals
Do not remove any stone, brick, or artifact fragment as a souvenir from any archaeological site
entries.penalty: Arrest, prosecution under heritage protection law

entries.title: Hire a licensed site guide at major Cultural Triangle sites - unlicensed 'guides' at Sigiriya/Polonnaruwa are a common tourist scam
Avoid casual photography near military camps, checkpoints, or memorial sites without asking first
entries.explanation: The North retains a significant military presence and unresolved war-memory sensitivities; casual photos are easily misread.

entries.title: Discuss the civil war and its history factually and respectfully - this remains a live, sensitive topic for local families
Some former conflict-zone areas still carry landmine-clearance signage - never leave marked paths in these areas
entries.explanation: Demining is largely complete in tourist areas but not universally; always respect posted signage.

entries.title: Cover shoulders/legs and remove footwear at Hindu kovils; leather items may be restricted inside
entries.explanation: Jaffna's Nallur Kandaswamy Kovil and other major kovils enforce dress code strictly, especially during festivals.

entries.title: Kataragama is sacred to Buddhists, Hindus, and Muslims simultaneously - dress and behave conservatively across the whole complex
During Ramadan, avoid eating, drinking, or smoking in public in heavily Muslim areas out of respect for those fasting
Women should carry a headscarf for mosque visits; men should wear long trousers
Agree on tuk-tuk/taxi fares before the ride, or insist on the meter - rate scams are common near tourist sites
Be cautious of unsolicited 'guides' or 'gem shop' referrals near major attractions - commission-driven scams are common
Verify any 'government closure' or 'special ticket' claims made by street touts at temples/sites directly at the official ticket counter
Do not deface, burn, or mishandle the Sri Lankan national flag
Stand respectfully if the national anthem is played at a public event
Obtain an ETA/eVisa before arrival (or on-arrival where offered) - entering without valid authorization is an offense
entries.explanation: Most nationalities need an Electronic Travel Authorization before boarding. Verify the current scheme on immigration.gov.lk as rules changed several times in 2024-2025.

entries.title: Do not overstay your visa - overstays incur fines and can lead to detention and future entry bans
entries.penalty: Fine per period overstayed, detention, entry ban

entries.title: Working (including remote-style paid gigs for local clients, guiding, or teaching) on a tourist visa is prohibited
Carry your passport or a photo/copy of it - police can ask for identification
entries.explanation: A phone photo of the data page plus visa is accepted for routine checks; keep the original in the hotel safe.

entries.title: Visa extensions are processed at the Department of Immigration in Battaramulla, Colombo - apply before expiry
Exporting antiques older than about 100 years without a license is prohibited
entries.explanation: Covers old masks, palm-leaf manuscripts, coins, carvings and colonial-era items under the Antiquities Ordinance.

entries.title: Buying or exporting coral, turtle-shell, or ivory products is illegal
entries.explanation: Protected under wildlife and CITES rules - refuse these souvenirs even when openly offered in shops.

entries.title: Protected timber (ebony, sandalwood) items may not be exported without permits
Satellite phones and some radio equipment require Telecommunications Regulatory Commission approval to bring in
Gems bought for export should carry a National Gem & Jewellery Authority certificate - keep receipts for customs
Do not take sand, shells in bulk, or beach coral fragments home
entries.explanation: Removal of beach material from protected coastal zones is restricted; small ordinary shells are tolerated, bags of them are not.

entries.title: Personal tea purchases are fine to export; commercial quantities need paperwork
South-west monsoon (roughly May-September): west and south coast seas are dangerous - swim on the east coast instead
entries.explanation: Rip currents and heavy shore break kill swimmers every season at Mirissa, Unawatuna, Hikkaduwa and Bentota during these months.

entries.title: North-east monsoon (roughly November-March): east coast seas turn rough - Trincomalee/Arugam Bay swimming is safest May-September
Most beaches have no lifeguards - a red flag means do not swim, and no flag does not mean safe
Caught in a rip current: swim parallel to the beach until free, then in - never fight it straight back
Never swim in river mouths, estuaries or lagoons - saltwater crocodiles are present
entries.explanation: Crocodile attacks occur at river mouths near Bentota, the Nilwala river, Pottuvil lagoon and southern estuaries.

entries.title: Never swim at night or after drinking alcohol - the most common factor in tourist drownings
Wear reef shoes on rocky/reef flats - sea urchins and sharp coral cause most beach injuries
Seasonal jellyfish blooms occur - ask locals before swimming if you see others staying out of the water
Insist on life jackets for any boat trip, lagoon crossing or snorkelling tour
entries.explanation: Operator safety standards vary widely; a licensed operator provides jackets without argument.

entries.title: Surf etiquette applies at Arugam Bay, Weligama and Hikkaduwa - don't drop in on locals' waves, keep your leash on
Kitesurfing at Kalpitiya: stay in designated kite zones and clear of swimmers - lines are dangerous
Pigeon Island National Park: tickets required, no touching coral, no feeding fish, and weak swimmers should wear a life jacket
Helmets are mandatory for motorbike riders AND passengers
Drink-driving is enforced with breath tests - the legal limit is low, treat it as zero
Front seatbelts are mandatory; using a handheld phone while driving is an offense
Expressways (E01/E02/E03) ban tuk-tuks, motorbikes, bicycles and pedestrians
Vehicles rarely stop at pedestrian crossings - cross defensively, make eye contact
Drive with extreme care at dusk/night on the Habarana-Polonnaruwa-Kataragama corridors: wild elephants cross the road
entries.explanation: Do not stop next to a roadside elephant, keep windows up, and never feed them from vehicles - it trains them to block cars.

entries.title: Unlit vehicles, dogs and cattle on rural roads make night driving genuinely hazardous - avoid long night drives
On buses, front seats are reserved for clergy - vacate them for monks without being asked
Reserve Kandy-Ella train seats well in advance; unreserved carriages are crush-loaded in season
entries.explanation: Keep daypacks on your front in crowded carriages - petty theft happens at peak crowding.

entries.title: Use metered ride apps (PickMe/Uber) in cities rather than flagging tuk-tuks at tourist hotspots
Prefer day trains over overnight buses on hill routes - bus speeding on mountain bends is a known accident cause
Dengue is present year-round island-wide - use repellent, especially at dawn/dusk in monsoon months
entries.explanation: Urban Colombo and the wet-zone west coast see the largest outbreaks; there is no vaccine generally available to travellers.

entries.title: Tap water is not for drinking - use bottled, filtered or boiled water everywhere
Any dog/monkey bite or scratch needs immediate rabies post-exposure treatment - go to a hospital the same day
entries.explanation: Street dogs are everywhere and rabies is endemic; government hospitals give post-exposure vaccination.

entries.title: Monkeys at Dambulla, Mihintale, Sigiriya and temple sites snatch food, bottles and glasses - keep bags zipped, don't feed them
Temple courtyards are walked barefoot - stone burns feet at midday, so visit early morning or evening
Hill country nights are genuinely cold (Nuwara Eliya can approach 5°C) - pack warm layers
Heat and humidity on the lowland plains cause heatstroke - hydrate and avoid midday exertion, especially April-May
Choose registered Ayurveda spas/practitioners - unregistered massage parlours are a known problem sector
Private hospitals expect payment guarantees/deposits - carry travel insurance details
Give and receive items (money, gifts, food) with your right hand or both hands, never the left alone
Never touch anyone's head, including children's
Don't point at people with an index finger - gesture with the whole hand
Remove shoes when entering homes
Ask before photographing people, especially women, market vendors and fishing crews
The 'stilt fishermen' near Koggala are largely a posed photo attraction - agree a price BEFORE taking pictures
entries.explanation: Payment demands after unsolicited photos are a routine dispute; genuine stilt fishing is now rare.

entries.title: Avoid photographing school children or school grounds
Don't give money or sweets to begging children - it fuels school-skipping; donate to registered charities instead
Losing your temper in public loses the argument - stay calm and quiet in any dispute and you will be helped
Swimwear belongs on the beach only - cover up in shops, buses and towns even in beach resorts
Tipping ~10% is appreciated where no service charge is added; round up tuk-tuk fares
Bargaining is normal in markets and with tuk-tuks, not in supermarkets, pharmacies or metered taxis
The side-to-side head wobble generally means 'yes/okay', not 'no'
Kandy Esala Perahera (July/August): stay behind barriers, never cross the procession route, no flash at the elephants
entries.explanation: Arrive hours early for a spot; dress modestly; watch for pickpockets in the crush.

entries.title: Vesak week (May): expect road closures around lantern displays; dansal (free food stalls) welcome everyone - queue politely, take modest portions
Poson Poya (June): Anuradhapura and Mihintale fill with hundreds of thousands of pilgrims - book rooms far ahead, dress white/modest
Kataragama festival (July/August): keep a respectful distance from fire-walking and kavadi rituals - never imitate or obstruct them
Nallur Festival, Jaffna (Aug, ~25 days): strict kovil dress code - men enter the inner precinct bare-chested, no leather
Sinhala & Tamil New Year (April 13-14): the whole country effectively closes for days - book transport and rooms early
entries.explanation: Trains/buses are packed before and after; many restaurants and shops shut. If invited to village games, join - it's the point.

entries.title: Thai Pongal (January): don't step on kolam (rice-flour designs) drawn at doorways in Tamil areas
Flying drones over any perahera/procession or festival crowd is prohibited without clearance
Duruthu Perahera at Kelaniya Temple (January) draws huge crowds just outside Colombo - same conduct rules as Kandy's perahera
South-west monsoon (May-September): flooding and landslides hit Ratnapura, Kalutara, Kegalle and Galle districts
entries.explanation: Check NBRO landslide warnings before hill-road journeys in heavy rain; rivers rise fast.

entries.title: North-east monsoon (December-February): flooding affects Trincomalee, Batticaloa and Ampara lowlands
Inter-monsoon months (October-November, March-April): violent afternoon thunderstorms island-wide - lightning kills people every year
entries.explanation: Shelter in buildings, not under trees, and get off open water and beaches when storms build.

entries.title: Adam's Peak climbing season is December-May only - the off-season trail is unlit, wet and effectively unsupported
Hill-country roads (Ella, Haputale, Nuwara Eliya) are landslide-prone after sustained rain - check road status before travelling
Sinharaja and the wet-zone rainforests rain most afternoons - trek mornings, and expect leeches year-round
Yala National Park typically closes for several weeks around September-October for drought - plan safaris accordingly
Minneriya/Kaudulla 'Gathering' of elephants peaks August-September in the dry season
Northern plains (Vavuniya, Mannar, Jaffna) hit dangerous heat indexes in April-May - plan around midday
Rare but real: late-year cyclonic storms can affect the north and east coasts (November-December) - heed Met Department warnings
Knuckles Range weather flips fast from sun to zero-visibility mist - take a guide and warm/wet-weather layers
Nuwara Eliya gets ground frost on December-February mornings - the only place in Sri Lanka you'll need it, pack accordingly
Matale/Kandy 'spice garden' free tours end in heavy-pressure sales at inflated prices - buy spices at ordinary shops
At the airport, use official taxi counters or ride apps - ignore freelance touts in arrivals
'The temple/attraction is closed today, come to my shop instead' is a classic redirection scam - verify at the entrance yourself
Check guide licenses: legitimate guides carry SLTDA (tourist board) ID cards
Count your change: worn LKR notes of different values look similar, and short-changing tourists happens
The 'buy gems here, resell at home for profit' pitch is always a scam - tourist-grade stones have no resale margin
'Buy milk powder for my baby' redirections to a shop (where the shop later splits the refund) is a known Colombo/Galle scam
Persistent 'beach boys' selling tours/massages on southern beaches: a firm, polite no and walking on works
Use ATMs attached to banks; exchange money only at banks or licensed changers and keep the receipt
entries.explanation: Street-rate exchange offers are either counterfeit or short-count risks, and receipts are needed to reconvert rupees on exit.

entries.title: Keep bags worn on your front in Pettah market and on crowded buses
Temple of the Tooth: expect airport-style bag checks; no shorts, no sleeveless tops; busiest at puja times
Sri Maha Bodhi: white or light clothing preferred; never climb the enclosure walls or platforms around the sacred tree
entries.explanation: This is the world's oldest documented planted tree and Sri Lanka's most heavily guarded religious site.

entries.title: Mihintale is climbed barefoot on 1,840 rock-cut steps - go at dawn or late afternoon to avoid burnt feet
Sigiriya: hornet colonies live on the rock face - keep silent on the upper stairways and obey wardens if a swarm warning sounds
entries.explanation: Hornet attacks have hospitalized dozens of visitors at once; noise and vibration are known triggers.

entries.title: Sigiriya's Mirror Wall: touching or scratching graffiti onto the ancient plaster is an offense
Gal Vihara (Polonnaruwa): shoes AND hats off even though the Buddha statues stand in the open air
Koneswaram Kovil, Trincomalee: dress code enforced; take care near Swami Rock cliff edges
Colombo's Red Mosque (Jami Ul-Alfar) welcomes visitors outside prayer times - modest dress, women bring a headscarf
Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara: an active major temple, not a tourist site - follow worshipper flow, silence inside the image house
Ella Rock and Little Adam's Peak: informal 'guides' may claim the path is closed to sell services - the trails are free and open
Udawalawe: never feed elephants over the electric fence at the park boundary road
entries.explanation: Fence-line feeding created dangerous begging behaviour; authorities prohibit it and remove roadside fruit stalls.

entries.title: Sinharaja Forest Reserve requires a licensed guide - entry without one is not permitted
entries.explanation: UNESCO World Heritage rainforest; guides are assigned at the ticket office.

entries.title: Do not pick plants, orchids or seeds in any reserve - endemic flora is protected
Gal Oya boat safaris: wear the life jacket; keep hands inside near swimming elephants
Bundala and Kumana: bird-nesting areas require silence and distance - no drones, no playback calls
When Minneriya floods, the elephant gathering shifts to Kaudulla/Hurulu - your jeep operator choosing the park is normal
Mangrove boat tours (Madu Ganga, Kalpitiya): don't touch monitor lizards/birds and never let guides feed wildlife for photos
Beach bonfires need the property owner's/local council's okay - embers and leftover glass injure morning walkers
Take used batteries and broken electronics home - island e-waste handling is limited
Carry a refillable bottle - refill stations and boiled-water refills at guesthouses beat buying plastic bottles daily
Delft Island's wild ponies are protected - don't feed, chase or try to ride them
Ferries to Delft/Nainativu: take morning sailings and wear the life jacket - afternoon seas get rough
Nainativu hosts a major kovil AND the Nagadeepa Buddhist temple - dress for both, as pilgrims visit the two together
No posed/smiling selfies at war memorial and mass-grave sites - treat them like any war cemetery
Avoid political demonstrations around Galle Face, Fort and Lipton Circus - gatherings can turn confrontational and police respond hard
entries.explanation: Foreigners photographing or joining protests attract police attention.

entries.title: Casinos are legal (foreigners welcome) - bring your passport for entry
Galle Face Green at sunset is safe and family-filled - normal crowd awareness for phones/wallets applies
Sit lower than a monk when talking with one; don't hand things directly to a monk if you are a woman - place items down instead
Walking meditation paths and sand courtyards at forest monasteries are raked ritual spaces - walk around, not across
Socks are allowed where shoes are not - bring a pair for hot stone courtyards
Ridi Viharaya and Athugala (Elephant Rock) Kurunegala: standard temple dress and shoe rules apply; the rock climb is hot after 9am
White-water rafting at Kitulgala: use operators with helmets, jackets and certified guides - water levels swing fast after rain
Negombo beach and lagoon: fishing fleet areas are workplaces - don't handle catch/gear or block boat launches for photos
Polhena/Mirissa snorkelling: don't stand on the reef or chase turtles - give them 3m and never block their surfacing path
Batticaloa lagoon 'singing fish' boat trips: agree prices ahead; life jackets for night trips
Mannar's baobab trees and donkeys are local heritage - don't carve initials or feed the donkeys junk food
Vavuniya remains a garrison town - keep cameras away from the camps along the A9 road
War relics displayed along the A9 (tank, water tower) may be photographed; active military areas beside them may not - when unsure, ask
Hot springs at Kanniya: bathing is bucket-style over the wells, modest swimwear, no soap in the wells
Ticket checks are separate per site cluster - keep your Sacred City ticket on you all day
Cycling is the standard way around Polonnaruwa's ruins - dismount and park at shrine platforms, don't ride across them
Ravana Falls: rocks above the pools are slick and people have fallen - swim only in the lower pools, never climb the falls
Gregory Lake and Moon Plains: boating/quad operators vary in standards - life jackets and helmets are yours to insist on
Hambantota's salt pans and Bundala boundary roads are working/protected areas - stay on public roads for flamingo photos
Arugam Bay is relaxed but the villages behind it are conservative Muslim communities - cover up away from the beach strip
Kalutara Bodhiya: drivers honk/stop to donate at the roadside till - if driving, don't block the flow; visitors welcome, shoes off on the platform
Galle Fort ramparts: no barriers on the walls - watch children near edges, and don't jump into the sea with the local divers
entries.explanation: The famous rampart jumpers know the tides and rocks; tourists imitating them have been seriously hurt.

entries.title: Jaffna is more conservative than the south - shorts are fine for tourists but beachwear belongs only at the beach, and PDA draws stares
Wilpattu buffer villages: leopard sightings on roads at night are real - don't walk unlit rural roads after dark
